elements.sent-2411-001: This paper introduces CKTSO {(abbreviation of ``circuit solver'') , a novel sparse linear solver specially designed for the simulation program with integrated circuit emphasis (SPICE).}
elements.sent-2411-002: CKTSO is a parallel solver and can be run on a multi-core, shared-memory computer.
elements.sent-2411-003: The algorithms of CKTSO are designed by considering the features of matrices involved in SPICE simulations.
elements.sent-2411-004: CKTSO is superior to existing similar solvers mainly in the following three aspects.
elements.sent-2411-005: First, the matrix ordering step of CKTSO combines different types of ordering algorithms such that it can generally obtain the fewest fill-ins for a wide range of circuit matrices.
elements.sent-2411-006: Second, CKTSO provides a parallel fast LU factorization algorithm with pivot check, which behaves good performance, scalability, and numerical stability.
elements.sent-2411-007: Third, CKTSO provides a structure-adaptive hybrid parallel triangular solving algorithm, which can adapt to various circuit matrices.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experiments including both benchmark tests and SPICE simulations demonstrate the superior performance of CKTSO.
elements.sent-2411-009: The libraries of CKTSO are available at https://github.com/chenxm1986/cktso.
elements.sent-2411-010: SPICE utilizes numerical computation theories to find the detailed responses of integrated circuits.
elements.sent-2411-011: A SPICE simulation process involves solving a series of sparse linear systems.
elements.sent-2411-012: Fig.~\ref{fig:tranflow illustrates a typical transient simulation flow.}
elements.sent-2411-013: There are two nested levels of loops, where the outer loop iterates the time and a Newton-Raphson (NR) iteration is performed in each inner loop.
elements.sent-2411-014: In each NR iteration, a sparse linear system (${\bf Ax ={\bf b}$ where $\bf A$ is sparse) is solved.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The linear solver is usually the performance bottleneck of SPICE simulators.
elements.sent-2411-016: In post-layout simulations, the linear solver can spend more than half of the total simulation time~\cite{daniels_hspice_tech_2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: SPICE simulators generally use LU factorization~\cite{davis2016survey to solve sparse linear systems, as linear systems in SPICE simulations are usually not well conditioned.}
elements.sent-2411-018: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:tranflow , sparse LU factorization involves three main steps: pre-processing, numerical factorization, and triangular solving.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Pre-processing reorders the matrix to minimize fill-ins that will be generated during numerical factorization.
elements.sent-2411-020: It is performed only once if the matrix structure is fixed, which usually holds in SPICE simulations.
elements.sent-2411-021: In each NR iteration, the matrix is factorized into LU factors: ${\bf A ={\bf LU}$, where $\bf L$ is a lower-triangular matrix and $\bf U$ is an upper-triangular matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Finally, the solution $\bf x$ is obtained by solving two triangular systems, ${\bf Ly}\!=\!\bf b$ and ${\bf Ux}\!=\!\bf y$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Numerical factorization and triangular solving are executed in every NR iteration.
elements.sent-2411-024: Typically, numerical factorization spends more time than triangular solving.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.45\columnwidth]{tranflow} \caption{Sparse solver in a typical transient simulation flow.}\label{fig:tranflow}
elements.sent-2411-025: KLU~\cite{davis_klu_toms_2010 , developed in 2006, is a sequential implementation of the sparse left-looking algorithm~\cite{gilbert_gp_jssc_1988 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: NICSLU~\cite{tcad13,tcasii , developed in 2013, parallelizes the sparse left-looking algorithm at the column granularity.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Later in 2016, Basker~\cite{basker is developed, which partitions the matrix and parallelizes sparse LU factorization at the sub-matrix granularity.}
elements.sent-2411-028: By exploring parallelism in the sparse left-looking algorithm, both NICSLU and Basker achieve speedups relative to KLU.
elements.sent-2411-029: PARDISO~\cite{pardiso , which is a general-purpose sparse direct solver, has also shown good performance in semiconductor device simulations~\cite{pardiso2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Despite the good performance of these solvers, it has been found that they have issues when facing some type of circuits.
elements.sent-2411-031: Various circuits put high demands for every step of LU factorization-based sparse linear solvers.
elements.sent-2411-032: Challenge 1: The popular matrix ordering methods, minimum degree and its variants~\cite{amd,mf,mmd , which are adopted by KLU and NICSLU, usually do not perform well on post-layout, mesh-style, or very large circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Instead, nested dissection~\cite{nd generally obtains better orderings on such matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, nested dissection performs badly on pre-layout or small circuits.
elements.sent-2411-035: Challenge 2: The high sparsity of circuit matrices leads to low parallelism and a low computation-to-communication ratio in numerical LU factorization, which is difficult to be efficiently parallelized on multi-core processors.
elements.sent-2411-036: Challenge 3: For triangular solving, the parallelism and computation-to-communication ratio are both extremely low, and it is extremely difficult to get speedups by parallelism.
elements.sent-2411-037: NICSLU provides parallel factorization but the solving step is sequential.
elements.sent-2411-038: This paper is extended from \cite{cktso and introduces a complete solver, CKTSO (abbreviation of ``circuit solver''), to accelerate circuit simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-039: CKTSO is developed by redesigning the three steps of sparse solvers for SPICE simulators, to address all the above-mentioned challenges.
elements.sent-2411-040: CKTSO has the following three unique features.
elements.sent-2411-041: The three features are also the major technical advances of CKTSO relative to NICSLU.
elements.sent-2411-042: The contributions of this work are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-043: A parallel fast LU factorization algorithm is proposed, which combines the advantages of both factorization with pivoting and re-factorization without pivoting, and behaves good performance and scalability, as well as good numerical stability.
elements.sent-2411-044: A structure-adaptive hybrid parallel triangular solving method is proposed.
elements.sent-2411-045: It adaptively splits the triangular matrices (i.e., $\bf L$ and $\bf U$) and uses different parallel triangular solving strategies, according to the characteristics of the symbolic structures of the sub-matrices.
elements.sent-2411-046: Experimental results have revealed that CKTSO achieves better performance for a wide range of circuit matrices than stat-of-the-art solvers.
elements.sent-2411-047: In practical SPICE simulations, CKTSO also shows better performance in different simulation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-048: The sparse left-looking LU factorization~\cite{gilbert_gp_jssc_1988 is widely adopted by linear solvers for SPICE simulations (e.g., KLU~\cite{davis_klu_toms_2010 , NICSLU~\cite{tcad13,tcasii , and Basker~\cite{basker ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: It computes the LU factors column by column.
elements.sent-2411-050: CKTSO uses the row-major order, so the left-looking algorithm is transposed and can be named as sparse up-looking LU factorization, as shown in Algorithm~\ref{algo:leftlooking .}
elements.sent-2411-051: It computes the LU factors row by row (line 1).
elements.sent-2411-052: As its name implies, when computing a row, some rows on its upper side will be ``looked at'' (used).
elements.sent-2411-053: When computing a row, symbolic prediction (line 2), numerical update (lines 3-5), and pivoting (lines 6-7) are executed.
elements.sent-2411-054: Since pivoting may change the structure of the LU factors, symbolic prediction cannot be decoupled from numerical computation.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Sparse up-looking LU factorization algorithm~\cite{gilbert_gp_jssc_1988}. If lines 2, 6, and 7 are removed, it becomes the up-looking LU re-factorization algorithm.}\label{algo:leftlooking} \KwIn{$N\times N$ matrix $\bf A$ after pre-processing, pivoting threshold $\varepsilon$} \KwOut{Matrices $\bf L$ and $\bf U$} \For {$i=1:N$} { Symbolic prediction: find symbolic structures of ${\bf L}(i,1\!:\!i)$ and ${\bf U}(i,i\!:\!N)$\; ${\bf x}={\bf A}(i,:)$\; \tcc{ $\bf x$ is a vector of length $N$ for holding intermediate results of current row} \For {$j=1:i-1$ {where} ${\bf L}(i,j)$ { is nonzero}} { ${\bf x}(j+1:N)-={\bf x}(j)\times {\bf U}(j,j+1:N)$\; } \If {$|{\bf x}(i)|<\varepsilon \times \mathop {\max }\limits_{i + 1 \le k \le N} \left\{ {|{\bf{x}}(k)|} \right\}$} { Exchange ${\bf x}(i)$ with ${\bf x}(\mathop {\arg \max }\limits_{i + 1 \le k \le N} \left\{ {|{\bf{x}}(k)|} \right\})$\; } ${\bf L}(i,1:i)={\bf x}(1:i)$\; ${\bf U}(i,i:N)={\bf x}(i:N)/{\bf x}(i)$\; }
elements.sent-2411-055: Symbolic prediction (line 2) determines the symbolic structure of the current row, based on the already computed rows on the upper side~\cite{gilbert_gp_jssc_1988,davis_klu_toms_2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: The symbolic structure of ${\bf L (i,1:i-1)$ also determines which rows on the upper side will update the current row.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Numerical update (lines 3-5) uses these dependent rows to update the values of the current row.
elements.sent-2411-058: The core operation of numerical update is a series of floating-point multiply-and-accumulates (MACs) (line 5).
elements.sent-2411-059: After that, pivoting (lines 6-7) is performed to ensure large diagonal elements.
elements.sent-2411-060: When the diagonal element is smaller than the maximum element in the current row of $\bf U$ times the pivoting threshold ($\varepsilon$), the diagonal element and the largest element in the current row of $\bf U$ are exchanged (line 7).
elements.sent-2411-061: The above described flow is a complete sparse up-looking LU factorization with pivoting.
elements.sent-2411-062: Before LU factorization, if the diagonal elements are assumed to be large enough, pivoting can be skipped.
elements.sent-2411-063: As a result, the symbolic structure of the LU factors will not change, and symbolic prediction can also be eliminated.
elements.sent-2411-064: In this case, the algorithm becomes re-factorization without pivoting, which only performs numerical update for each row.
elements.sent-2411-065: Re-factorization reuses the symbolic structure and pivoting order obtained in the last factorization with pivoting.
elements.sent-2411-066: If one or more small diagonal elements that do not meet the pivoting rule appear in re-factorization, they cannot be handled and may cause large errors in the solution.
elements.sent-2411-067: To perform parallel up-looking LU factorization, dependencies between rows should be first determined.
elements.sent-2411-068: The sparsity of the LU factors implies the inter-row parallelism.
elements.sent-2411-069: Lines 4-5 of Algorithm~\ref{algo:leftlooking indicate that row $i$ depends on row $j$ (i.e., row $i$ needs row $j$'s values for numerical update) if and only if ${\bf L (i,j)$ is a nonzero element.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In short, the symbolic structure of $\bf L$ determines the inter-row dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-071: Based on this principle, a dependency graph can be built based on the symbolic structure of $\bf L$.
elements.sent-2411-072: There is an edge $j\to i$ in the dependency graph if ${\bf L (i,j)$ is a nonzero element.}
elements.sent-2411-073: This dependency graph can be named as an elimination graph (EGraph).
elements.sent-2411-074: The EGraph describes exact inter-row dependencies corresponding to a specific symbolic structure of the LU factors, and can be used to schedule parallel LU re-factorization without pivoting~\cite{tcasii .}
elements.sent-2411-075: For parallel factorization with pivoting, however, it is impossible to determine the exact inter-row dependencies prior to factorization, since the symbolic structure of $\bf L$ depends on pivoting and is known only after factorization is completed.
elements.sent-2411-076: To solve this dilemma, people have proposed a concept of elimination tree (ETree)~\cite{liu_etree_jmaa_1990 , which describes an upper bound of the inter-row dependencies when considering pivoting.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The meaning of the upper bound is that, the dependencies generated by any pivoting order of up-looking factorization are contained in the ETree.
elements.sent-2411-078: The ETree is constructed from the symbolic structure of matrix $\bf A$ {(if $\bf A$ is unsymmetric, the ETree is built from ${\bf A ^\mathsf{T {\bf A}$ without explicitly forming ${\bf A ^\mathsf{T {\bf A}$~\cite{liu_etree_jmaa_1990 ) , so it can be built in the pre-processing step.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-079: The EGraph and ETree are both directed acyclic graphs, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:example .}
elements.sent-2411-080: The parallelism implied in EGraph and ETree can be explored by levelizing them, so that parallel factorization and re-factorization can both be scheduled by a unified method~\cite{tcad13,tcasii .}
elements.sent-2411-081: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:para , the EGraph or ETree can be levelized (Fig.~\ref{fig:para (a)) so that nodes (a node corresponds to a row) in the same level have no dependency.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The level of a node is the maximum path length from any source node to itself.
elements.sent-2411-083: A dual-mode scheduling method has been proposed~\cite{tcad13,tcasii .}
elements.sent-2411-084: For front levels that have a large number of nodes in each level, they are computed level by level.
elements.sent-2411-085: Nodes in a level are evenly assigned to threads and computed in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-086: This method is called a cluster mode.
elements.sent-2411-087: For the remaining levels that have very few nodes in each level, a pipeline mode is proposed which explores finer-grained parallelism between dependent rows.
elements.sent-2411-088: Fig.~\ref{fig:para (d) illustrates an example of computing node 10 using the pipeline mode, corresponding to the dependencies described in Fig.~\ref{fig:para (a).}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Nodes 9 and 10 are computed in parallel in the pipeline mode.
elements.sent-2411-090: Since node 10 depends on node 9, node 10 can first use already-finished nodes (i.e., nodes 7 and 8) to perform partial updates.
elements.sent-2411-091: These partial updates are performed in parallel with the computation of node 9.
elements.sent-2411-092: This is the key to explore parallelism between dependent rows.
elements.sent-2411-093: After node 9 is finished, node 10 can use node 9's results to perform a partial update.
elements.sent-2411-094: The levelization-based dual-mode scheduling method has been adopted by NICSLU.
elements.sent-2411-095: The height and width of the ETree/EGraph can be an intuitive estimation of the scalability of parallel LU factorization/re-factorization.
elements.sent-2411-096: The height (maximum level) is the critical path length and the width (maximum number of nodes in each level) is the maximum parallelism.
elements.sent-2411-097: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:example , the ETree is tall and narrow, while the EGraph is short and wide.}
elements.sent-2411-098: As the ETree describes an inter-row dependency upper bound, it implies many redundant dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-099: We have tested more than 50 circuit matrices from the SuiteSparse Matrix Collection~\cite{davis_matrix_web .}
elements.sent-2411-100: On average, the ETree is nearly 80$\times$ taller than the EGraph, while the EGraph is 10$\times$ wider.
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, the scalability of parallel factorization with pivoting (scheduled by the ETree) is much poorer than that of re-factorization without pivoting (scheduled by the EGraph).
elements.sent-2411-102: According to the results in Ref.~\cite{book , for NICSLU, 16-threaded factorization with pivoting achieves only 2$\times$ speedup on average, compared with sequential factorization.}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Lower triangular solving of ${\bf Ly}=\bf b$.}\label{algo:solve} \KwIn{Matrix $\bf L$ obtained from LU factorization, vector $\bf b$} \KwOut{Vector $\bf y$} ${\bf y}={\bf b}$\; \For {$i=1:N$} { $s=0$\; \For {$j=1:i-1$ where ${\bf L}(i,j)$ is nonzero} { $s+={\bf y}(j)\times {\bf L}(i,j)$\; } ${\bf y}(i)=({\bf y}(i)-s)/{\bf L}(i,i)$\; }
elements.sent-2411-103: Algorithm~\ref{algo:solve shows the lower triangular solving of ${\bf Ly =\bf b$.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: With the row-major order, solving is also performed in a row-by-row manner (line 2).
elements.sent-2411-105: For each row, it traverses the nonzero elements of the current row of $\bf L$ (line 4), performs a MAC operation for each nonzero element (line 5), and finally computes an element of the solution vector (line 6).
elements.sent-2411-106: The upper triangular solving of ${\bf Ux =\bf y$ is similar, with the only difference that $\bf U$ is traversed in the reversed row order.}
elements.sent-2411-107: For solving ${\bf Ly =\bf b$, it can easily be seen that, if ${\bf L (i,j)$ ($j<i$) is a nonzero element, the solving of ${\bf y (i)$ depends on ${\bf y (j)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Based on this principle, one may also build a dependency graph to parallelize triangular solving.
elements.sent-2411-109: However, unlike LU factorization and re-factorization, triangular solving involves an extremely low computation-to-communication ratio.
elements.sent-2411-110: In fact, each nonzero element of $\bf L$ and $\bf U$ participates in exactly one MAC operation, and the primary bottleneck of triangular solving is not computation but memory access.
elements.sent-2411-111: As a result, parallelization is extremely difficult, since the memory access and inter-thread communication overheads caused by parallelization can be much larger than the computational workload.
elements.sent-2411-112: There are some popular general-purpose sparse solvers (e.g., SuperLU~\cite{slu , PARDISO~\cite{pardiso , UMFPACK~\cite{davis_umfpack_tms_2004 , et al.).}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: In recent years, KLU~\cite{davis_klu_toms_2010 , NICSLU~\cite{tcasii,tcad13 , {and Basker~\cite{basker are proposed to accelerate circuit simulations, which are based on the sparse left-looking algorithm~\cite{gilbert_gp_jssc_1988 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: The primary difference between the design principles of these circuit simulation-oriented solvers and general-purpose sparse solvers is due to the matrix sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-115: As matrices in circuit simulations are generally much sparser than matrices from other applications~\cite{davis_klu_toms_2010 , solvers designed for circuit simulations typically do not assemble nonzero elements to form big dense blocks which can be accelerated by dense linear algebra kernels.}
elements.sent-2411-116: KLU~\cite{davis_klu_toms_2010 is sequential while NICSLU~\cite{tcasii,tcad13 provides parallel left-looking LU factorization and re-factorization.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Factorization with pivoting and re-factorization without pivoting of NICSLU are scheduled by the ETree and EGraph, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-118: It is reported that NICSLU achieves better performance than other popular solvers (KLU, PARDISO, SuperLU, UMFPACK, etc.) in real circuit and power grid simulation problems~\cite{power_pes2015,power_2019,power2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: However, the scalability of NICSLU's factorization with pivoting is not so good (2$\times$ speedup using 8-16 threads)~\cite{book .}
elements.sent-2411-120: It partitions the matrix to a block triangular form where each diagonal block is further partitioned using nested dissection, generating a number of independent sub-matrices.
elements.sent-2411-121: It performs well on highly sparse matrices but does poorly on high fill-in density matrices (e.g., post-layout matrices).
elements.sent-2411-122: For triangular solving, current sparse solvers designed for circuit simulations mostly use sequential solving, including NICSLU which provides parallel factorization and re-factorization.
elements.sent-2411-123: Parallel triangular solving in general-purpose sparse solvers (e.g., \cite{bollhofer2020state,solve2 ) is typically based on some dependency analysis method which is similar to that used for parallel LU factorization.}
elements.sent-2411-124: However, this method is inefficient for highly-sparse circuit matrices, since there is little parallelizable workload and the scheduling overhead may be larger than the computational workload.
elements.sent-2411-125: The method behaves well for general matrices but may not be suitable for highly-sparse circuit matrices as the blocking scheme is too fine grained which causes heavy scheduling overhead.

elements.sent-2411-128: For pre-processing, {CKTSO first performs a static pivoting step based on the maximum weighted matching algorithm~\cite{staticpiv,staticpiv2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-129: The maximum weighted matching algorithm permutes large elements to the diagonal, such that the absolute value of the product of the diagonal elements is maximized.
elements.sent-2411-130: It greatly reduces the possibility of dynamic pivoting during numerical factorization (but dynamic pivoting cannot be completely avoided via static pivoting).
elements.sent-2411-131: It also calculates row and column scaling vectors that can scale the matrix values, such that the diagonal elements are 1 or -1, and non-diagonal elements are bounded in [-1,1].
elements.sent-2411-132: The scaling feature of CKTSO is controlled by users.
elements.sent-2411-133: Mathematically, static pivoting is to find a row permutation matrix $\bf P$ and two diagonal scaling matrices $\bf S_r$ and $\bf S_c$.
elements.sent-2411-134: The matrix after static pivoting is ${\bf A' =\bf PA$ (without scaling) or ${\bf A' =\bf S_rPAS_c$ (with scaling).}}
elements.sent-2411-135: After static pivoting, the matrix is reordered to minimize fill-ins that will be generated during numerical factorization.
elements.sent-2411-136: Mathematically, matrix ordering is to find a symmetric permutation matrix $\bf Q$, such that factorization of $\bf A''= QA'Q^\mathsf{T}$ will generate the minimum fill-ins.
elements.sent-2411-137: Finding an ordering with the minimum fill-ins is an NP-complete problem~\cite{np , so heuristics are used in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Minimum degree~\cite{amd,mf,mmd and nested dissection~\cite{nd are two popular kinds of matrix ordering methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Minimum degree is performed in an iterative manner and selects a node with the minimum degree in each iteration to be the pivot, and forms new fill-ins by eliminating the pivot.
elements.sent-2411-140: It is adopted by KLU~\cite{davis_klu_toms_2010 and NICSLU~\cite{tcasii,tcad13 , and generally performs well for small- to medium-scale matrices (e.g., pre-layout circuits).}}
elements.sent-2411-141: For large-scale matrices or matrices of post-layout circuits, nested dissection usually generates better orderings.
elements.sent-2411-142: One-level nested dissection partitions a graph which represents a matrix into three parts, a node separator whose size is minimized and two sub-graphs (Fig.~\ref{fig:nd (a)), such that removal of the node separator disconnects the two sub-graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-143: If the node separator is ordered last (Fig.~\ref{fig:nd (b)), no fill-in will occur between nodes of the two sub-graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Each sub-graph can be recursively ordered by the same method.
elements.sent-2411-145: Nested dissection is adopted by PARDISO~\cite{pardiso .}

elements.sent-2411-146: Since minimum degree and nested dissection respectively perform well on different types of matrices, CKTSO uses a combination of them.
elements.sent-2411-147: For minimum degree, CKTSO uses the popular approximate minimum degree algorithm~\cite{amd and its variant, the approximate minimum deficiency algorithm~\cite{mf .}}
elements.sent-2411-148: For nested dissection, though METIS~\cite{metis provides a widely-used implementation, it has some issues.}
elements.sent-2411-149: METIS recursively partitions a graph, until the sub-graphs' sizes are smaller than a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-150: Then, each sub-graph is ordered by the multiple minimum degree algorithm~\cite{mmd independently, and the node separator uses the natural order without ordering.}
elements.sent-2411-151: By using Fig.~\ref{fig:nd (b) to explain the issues, though the orderings of $\bf X$ and $\bf Y$ will not impact each other, they will impact the fill-ins of $\bf S$ through the two boundaries.}
elements.sent-2411-152: This implies that, when ordering $\bf X$ and $\bf Y$, their impacts on the fill-ins of $\bf S$ should be taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-153: This can be realized by applying a constrained minimum degree algorithm~\cite{cmd after the incomplete nested dissection.}
elements.sent-2411-154: The constrained minimum degree orders the entire matrix, where each pivot has a constraint of its group index and the pivots must be eliminated in the order of group.
elements.sent-2411-155: For example, in Fig.~\ref{fig:nd , the pivots in $\bf X$, $\bf Y$, and $\bf S$ are in groups 0, 1, and 2, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-156: By using the group to constrain the pivot elimination order, this method guarantees the sub-matrix-level order returned by the incomplete nested dissection, while all diagonal sub-matrices are properly ordered.
elements.sent-2411-157: CKTSO integrates a modified METIS (by removing the independent minimum degree ordering for each diagonal sub-matrix) followed by the constrained approximate minimum degree algorithm~\cite{cmd .}
elements.sent-2411-158: In the matrix ordering phase, CKTSO runs all the integrated ordering methods in parallel and selects the best with the minimum fill-ins.
elements.sent-2411-159: The combined ordering method can generally obtain the fewest fill-ins for a wide range of circuit matrices, and thus, addresses the challenge that a single ordering method may not be good for various circuit matrices.
elements.sent-2411-160: According to experiments, the pre-processing time of CKTSO is on average about 3$\times$ of that of NICSLU, and similar to that of PARDISO.
elements.sent-2411-161: Since pre-processing is a one-time procedure in a SPICE simulation, the pre-processing overhead is not so significant and can be hidden by the large number of more time-consuming NR iterations.
elements.sent-2411-162: CKTSO provides three LU factorization functions: factorization with pivoting, re-factorization without pivoting, and fast factorization with pivot check.
elements.sent-2411-163: They can be selected by users according to the scenario.
elements.sent-2411-164: The basic principles of the former two functions as well as their parallelization methodology are introduced in Section~\ref{sec:backg .}
elements.sent-2411-165: In fact, the factorization and re-factorization functions of CKTSO are same as those of NICSLU~\cite{tcasii,tcad13 , so they will not be introduced in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-166: CKTSO provides a novel parallel fast factorization algorithm by skipping pivoting but the numerical stability is maintained by pivot check.
elements.sent-2411-167: This algorithm will be introduced in Section~\ref{sec:factor .}
elements.sent-2411-168: CKTSO also provides a structure-adaptive hybrid parallel triangular solving algorithm, which will be introduced in Section~\ref{sec:solve .}
elements.sent-2411-169: In the rest of the paper, the notation of $\bf A$ will be used to denote the matrix after pre-processing.
elements.section-2411-004: Parallel Fast Factorization with Pivot Check
elements.sent-2411-170: The re-factorization without pivoting provided by KLU and NICSLU reuses the pivoting order and the symbolic structure of the LU factors obtained from the last factorization with pivoting, so symbolic-related operations can be eliminated.
elements.sent-2411-171: This is numerically unstable since the matrix values are changing during circuit simulation iterations, and disabling pivoting may generate erroneous solutions.
elements.sent-2411-172: To ensure correct solutions, factorization and re-factorization must be carefully selected in each circuit simulation iteration.
elements.sent-2411-173: However, this is not an easy task before numerical factorization.
elements.sent-2411-174: Evaluating whether the values of matrix $\bf A$ change much cannot give a stable prediction, as a small change in a few sensitive elements may change the LU factors' values dramatically.
elements.sent-2411-175: In circuit simulations, when the NR iterations of an operating-point (OP) simulation or at each time node in a transient simulation are near convergence, the matrix values tend to change smoothly, and successive NR iterations tend to reuse the previous pivoting order.
elements.sent-2411-176: This intuition provides an opportunity to ``guess'' the task dependencies before numerical factorization.
elements.sent-2411-177: If the structure of the LU factors and the pivoting order are aggressively assumed to be unchanged from the previous factorization, a ``guessed'' EGraph can be tentatively used to schedule parallel fast factorization, skipping symbolic prediction and pivoting, but the pivots should be checked.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Cluster mode of fast factorization with pivot check.}\label{algo:cluster} $interrupted=0$; \tcc{shared variable for all threads} \For{$L=1:L_{Cluster}$} { \For{threads in parallel} { \For {row $i$ assigned to this thread} { ${\bf x}={\bf A}(i,:)$\; \For {$j=1:i-1$ {where} ${\bf L}(i,j)$ { is nonzero}} { ${\bf x}(j+1\!:\!N)-={\bf x}(j)\times {\bf U}(j,j+1\!:\!N)$\; } \If {$|{\bf x}(i)|<\varepsilon \times \mathop {\max }\limits_{i + 1 \le k \le N} \left\{ {|{\bf{x}}(k)|} \right\}$} { $interrupted=1$\; continue\; } ${\bf L}(i,1:i)={\bf x}(1:i)$\; ${\bf U}(i,i:N)={\bf x}(i:N)/{\bf x}(i)$\; $ finish[i]=1$\; } } barrier\; \If{$interrupted$} { exit thread\; } }

elements.sent-2411-212: For each level, the nodes are evenly assigned to the threads and different threads compute the assigned nodes in parallel (line 3).
elements.sent-2411-213: For a specific node (row), numerical update (lines 5-7) is first performed just like re-factorization.

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Pipeline mode of fast factorization with pivot check.}\label{algo:pipeline} $interrupted=0$; \tcc{shared variable for all threads} \For{threads in parallel} { \For {row $i$ assigned to this thread} { ${\bf x}={\bf A}(i,:)$\; \For {$j=1:i-1$ {where} ${\bf L}(i,j)$ { is nonzero}} { \While {$!finish[j]$} { \If{$interrupted$} { exit thread\; } } ${\bf x}(j+1:N)-={\bf x}(j)\times {\bf U}(j,j+1:N)$\; } \If {$|{\bf x}(i)|<\varepsilon \times \mathop {\max }\limits_{i + 1 \le k \le N} \left\{ {|{\bf{x}}(k)|} \right\}$} { $interrupted=1$\; exit thread \; } ${\bf L}(i,1:i)={\bf x}(1:i)$\; ${\bf U}(i,i:N)={\bf x}(i:N)/{\bf x}(i)$\; $ finish[i]=1$\; } }

elements.sent-2411-223: The nodes belonging to the pipeline mode are first arranged to a sequence, and they are assigned to the threads on-the-fly.
elements.sent-2411-224: A pointer {\tt max\_busy is maintained which points to the maximum position in the node sequence which is being computed.}
elements.sent-2411-225: If a thread needs to get a new node to compute, it atomically increases {\tt max\_busy by 1 and fetches the resulting value.}
elements.sent-2411-226: The atomic operation guarantees that no two threads will get the same node.
elements.sent-2411-227: All threads compute the fetched nodes in parallel in an asynchronous way.

elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Pipelined tail factorization with pivoting.}\label{algo:tail} \For {threads in parallel} { \For {row $i$ assigned to this thread} { $\mathcal{U}=\emptyset$\; ${\bf x}={\bf A}(i,:)$\; \tcc{{pre-factorization}} \While {predecessors of $i$ not all finished} { $\mathcal{F}=\emptyset$\; Symbolic prediction, in which unfinished predecessors are skipped, and put detected dependent rows that are not in $\mathcal{U}$ into $\mathcal{F}$\; \For {$j\in \mathcal{F}$} { ${\bf x}(j+1\!:\!N)-={\bf x}(j)\times {\bf U}(j,j+1\!:\!N)$\; } $\mathcal{U}=\mathcal{U}\cup \mathcal{F}$\; } \tcc{{post-factorization}} Symbolic prediction: find symbolic structures of ${\bf L}(i,1:i)$ and ${\bf U}(i,i:N)$\; \For {$j=1:i-1$ {where} ${\bf L}(i,j)$ { is nonzero} and $j\notin \mathcal{U}$} { ${\bf x}(j+1:N)-={\bf x}(j)\times {\bf U}(j,j+1:N)$\; } \If {$|{\bf x}(i)|<\varepsilon \times \mathop {\max }\limits_{i + 1 \le k \le N} \left\{ {|{\bf{x}}(k)|} \right\}$} { Exchange ${\bf x}(i)$ with ${\bf x}(\mathop {\arg \max }\limits_{i + 1 \le k \le N} \left\{ {|{\bf{x}}(k)|} \right\})$\; } ${\bf L}(i,1:i)={\bf x}(1:i)$\; ${\bf U}(i,i:N)={\bf x}(i:N)/{\bf x}(i)$\; $ finish[i]=1$\; } }

elements.sent-2411-263: Although numerical factorization spends longer time than triangular solving, triangular solving may also become the performance bottleneck in SPICE simulators.
elements.sent-2411-264: For example, in a linear circuit simulation with a fixed time step, only one factorization step is needed but a number of triangular solving steps are performed.
elements.sent-2411-265: Triangular solving is extremely difficult to parallelize, because triangular solving is highly sequential and the computational workload is too small.
elements.sent-2411-266: As mentioned in Section~\ref{sec:back_solve , each nonzero element in $\bf L$ and $\bf U$ only participates in one MAC operation.}
elements.sent-2411-267: CPU threads are not suitable for scheduling such fine-grained computations, as the scheduling overhead will be much higher than the computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-268: To minimize the scheduling overhead, one should group the elements with some similar features such that they can be scheduled together.
elements.sent-2411-269: Problem partitioning is usually the key to explore parallelism and also to reduce parallel overhead.
elements.sent-2411-270: How to partition the triangular matrices becomes the most important challenge for parallelizing triangular solving.
elements.sent-2411-271: Using a single partitioning method for an entire triangular matrix (e.g., the method of Ref.~\cite{solve ) may not be the best solution, because the nonzero elements in $\bf L$ and $\bf U$ are not distributed uniformly.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Instead, their distribution has obvious features.
elements.sent-2411-273: The nonzero elements usually become denser on the right-bottom corner.
elements.sent-2411-274: This is due to the nature of LU factorization.
elements.sent-2411-275: This feature provides a possibility to partition the LU factors using different methods according to the nonzero element distribution so that different parallelism strategies can be used for different sub-matrices, according to their characteristics of the nonzero structure.
elements.sent-2411-276: By scheduling tasks at the sub-matrix level, the structure-adaptive method also reduces the scheduling overhead.
elements.sent-2411-277: In this section, the lower triangular solving will be used as an example to illustrate the parallel triangular solving methodology of CKTSO, and the upper triangular solving method is similar.

elements.sent-2411-278: To address the challenges of low parallelism and low computational workload of triangular solving, CKTSO employs a structure-adaptive hybrid parallel triangular solving method, which is based on a structure-adaptive triangular matrix partitioning method.
elements.sent-2411-279: Fig.~\ref{fig:dist draws a typical distribution of the nonzero elements of the LU factors.}
elements.sent-2411-280: As can be seen, the majority of the nonzero elements are located at the right-bottom corner, while there are two boundaries at the bottom and right sides, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-281: For large-scale circuit matrices, the size of the dense block at the right-bottom corner is usually several hundred to several thousand.
elements.sent-2411-282: Consequently, using the lower triangular matrix as an example, it can be partitioned into three blocks like Fig.~\ref{fig:partition (a), which are called a sparse triangular block, a rectangular block, and a dense triangular block, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-283: Since different matrices have diverse nonzero element distribution and sparsity, the partitioning position ($P_0$) should be adaptively determined.
elements.sent-2411-284: Considering that the dense triangular block dominates the total solving time, the partitioning position is mainly determined by considering the density of the dense triangular block.
elements.sent-2411-285: In CKTSO, the partitioning position ($P_0$) is adaptively determined by the following two rules.
elements.sent-2411-286: First, the dense triangular block should have at least 70\% of the nonzero elements of $\bf L$.
elements.sent-2411-287: Second, the dense triangular block should have at least 300,000 nonzero elements.
elements.sent-2411-288: The two thresholds are empirically determined based on lots of experiments.
elements.sent-2411-289: The two conditions guarantee that the dense triangular block is not too sparse or too small.
elements.sent-2411-290: If it is very small or sparse, its computational workload will be small but the scheduling overhead will dominate the solving time.
elements.sent-2411-291: If the two conditions cannot hold simultaneously, that is, the dense triangular block is too small or sparse, $\bf L$ is not partitioned and it is treated as a single sparse triangular block.
elements.sent-2411-292: The following contents will show how to further partition the rectangular and dense triangular blocks, if the two conditions can hold.
elements.sent-2411-293: The rectangular block is trivial to parallelize.
elements.sent-2411-294: However, the dense triangular block involves strong dependencies and it is almost impossible to be solved in parallel, because the rows are almost one-by-one dependent.
elements.sent-2411-295: To realize efficient parallel triangular solving, the rectangular block is further partitioned into several slices, and the dense triangular block is also further partitioned into rectangular slices and triangular pieces, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:partition (b).}
elements.sent-2411-296: To simplify the scheduling, the rectangular slices in the rectangular block and the triangular block are merged.
elements.sent-2411-297: The final partitioning scheme is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:partition (c).}

elements.sent-2411-335: The above described parallel triangular solving algorithm needs some setup operations.
elements.sent-2411-336: The setup mainly contains 4 operations: 1) triangular matrix partitioning, 2) levelization of the dependency graph of the sparse triangular block, 3) thread workload assignment, and 4) trapezoid slice segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-337: It should be noted that the dependency graph of the sparse triangular block needs not to be explicitly created, because the inter-row dependencies are implied in the symbolic structure of $\bf L$ and $\bf U$.
elements.sent-2411-338: The setup is needed only once for a specific symbolic structure of the LU factors.
elements.sent-2411-339: However, if the symbolic structure of the LU factors changes due to re-pivoting, the setup needs to be re-performed.
elements.sent-2411-340: Triangular matrix partitioning first determines the partitioning position $P_0$.
elements.sent-2411-341: This is done by traversing from $N-1$ to 1 to check the two conditions mentioned in Section~\ref{sec:partition .}
elements.sent-2411-342: If $P_0$ can be found, the partitioning positions $P_1,P_2,\cdots,P_{m-1}$ are further determined by averaging the nonzero elements from row $P_0+1$ to row $N$.
elements.sent-2411-343: This is simply done by traversing the rows and accumulating the row lengths (i.e., the numbers of nonzero elements of the rows).
elements.sent-2411-344: If the current accumulated sum is equal to or larger than the average value (i.e., the total number of nonzero elements from row $P_0+1$ to row $N$ divided by $m$), the accumulation stops and the currently traversed rows are in a trapezoid slice, and then a new accumulation process will start for the next trapezoid slice.
elements.sent-2411-345: The time complexity of triangular matrix partitioning is $O(N)$.

elements.sent-2411-346: Levelization of the dependency graph of the sparse triangular block needs to traverse the nonzero elements of the triangular matrix to calculate the levels of the rows.
elements.sent-2411-347: The time complexity is $O(NNZ)$, where $NNZ$ is the number of nonzero elements in the LU factors.
elements.sent-2411-348: Thread workload assignment uses a similar method to the determination of the partitioning positions $P_1,P_2,\cdots,P_{m-1}$, to ensure that each thread processes a similar number of nonzero elements.
elements.sent-2411-349: The time complexity of thread workload assignment is $O(N)$.
elements.sent-2411-350: Trapezoid slice segmentation can speed up the solving process for the trapezoid slices.
elements.sent-2411-351: The nonzero elements in each row of the trapezoid slices need to be divided into two segments, the rectangular slice and the triangular piece.
elements.sent-2411-352: However, since the column indexes of the LU factors are out of order in each row after the up-looking LU factorization, the segmentation is not trivial.
elements.sent-2411-353: Completely sorting each row is unnecessary, but for each row in trapezoid slice ${\bf R_k |{\bf T_k}$, the nonzero elements before and after $P_{k-1}$ need to be separated.}
elements.sent-2411-354: CKTSO uses an algorithm that is similar to a single step of quicksort.
elements.sent-2411-355: That is, each row is partitioned into two segments by swapping the elements, according to whether their column indexes are less than or greater than $P_{k-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-356: The time complexity of trapezoid slice segmentation is $O(NNZ)$.
elements.sent-2411-357: The segmentation of different rows can be trivially parallelized.
elements.sent-2411-358: In summary, the time complexity of the setup of triangular solving is $O(NNZ)$, which equals the time complexity of triangular solving.
elements.sent-2411-359: According to experiments, the setup time is about 2$\times$ of a solving time.
elements.sent-2411-360: Considering that the symbolic structure of the LU factors can be reused in several tens or more NR iterations in practical SPICE simulations, the setup overhead is negligible.
elements.sent-2411-361: CKTSO is compared with NICSLU~\cite{tcad13,tcasii and Intel oneMKL PARDISO~\cite{pardiso .}}
elements.sent-2411-362: NICSLU is a state-of-the-art sparse solver specially designed for circuit simulations, which demonstrates outstanding performance in real circuit and power grid simulation problems~\cite{power_pes2015,power_2019,power2015 , while PARDISO is a general-purpose sparse solver and generally performs the best among a number of popular sparse solvers for non-circuit matrices~\cite{compare .}}
elements.sent-2411-363: Experiments are run on a Linux server equipped with an Intel Xeon Gold 6130 CPU (16 cores, 2.1-3.7GHz, 22MB last-level cache) and 256GB memory.
elements.sent-2411-364: Fifty-six circuit matrices (dimensions from 2,904 to 5,558,326) from the SuiteSparse Matrix Collection~\cite{davis_matrix_web are tested.}
elements.sent-2411-365: General cases that incur re-pivoting are also tested.
elements.sent-2411-366: CKTSO is also compared with NICSLU in an in-house SPICE simulator for OP and transient simulations.
elements.sent-2411-367: The 56 benchmarks will be shown in the order of arithmetic density, which is defined to be $\frac{\#FLOPs {NNZ({\bf L +{\bf U -{\bf I )}$, where $\#FLOPs$ is the number of floating-point operations (FLOPs) involved in numerical LU factorization.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-369: NICSLU uses the approximate minimum degree algorithm~\cite{amd and its variants~\cite{mf , while PARDISO uses nested dissection implemented by METIS~\cite{metis .}}}
elements.sent-2411-370: On average, NICSLU and PARDISO generate 8.8\% and 62.3\% more fill-ins than CKTSO, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-371: Minimum degree performs well for most benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-372: Only for a few large-scale, post-layout, or mesh-style benchmarks, such as G3\_circuit, G2\_circuit, memchip, meg1, and FullChip, minimum degree generates significantly more fill-ins than nested dissection.
elements.sent-2411-373: Nested dissection adopted by PARDISO generates more fill-ins than minimum degree for most benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-374: On average, NICSLU and PARDISO generate 39.8\% and 188\% more FLOPs than CKTSO, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-375: This comparison reveals that CKTSO generally obtains the fewest fill-ins for a wide range of circuit matrices, by combing minimum degree and nested dissection methods.
elements.sent-2411-376: Fig.~\ref{fig:ftime compares CKTSO's fast factorization with NICSLU's and PARDISO's factorization.}
elements.sent-2411-377: It can be observed that CKTSO generally has the smallest factorization time.
elements.sent-2411-378: CKTSO is the fastest for 50 out of the 56 benchmarks, among the compared solvers.
elements.sent-2411-379: Due to the reduced fill-ins and FLOPs brought by the matrix ordering method, sequential CKTSO is even faster than parallel NICSLU and PARDISO for half of the benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-380: Fig.~\ref{fig:fscalability shows the scalability of CKTSO's fast factorization.}
elements.sent-2411-381: For the average speedup across the 56 benchmarks, the fast factorization is sped up by 1.27$\times$, 2.23$\times$, 3.5$\times$, and 4.86$\times$ when using 2, 4, 8, and 16 threads, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-382: The scalability tends to be better for matrices with higher arithmetic density.
elements.sent-2411-383: This is easy to understand, since with higher arithmetic density, the relative computational workload is larger and the parallel scheduling overhead is relatively smaller.
elements.sent-2411-384: When compared with parallel factorization of NICSLU and PARDISO, CKTSO's fast factorization achieves good speedups, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fspeedup .}
elements.sent-2411-385: CKTSO is consistently more than 9$\times$ faster than PARDISO on average, when both solvers use 1, 4, 8, and 16 threads, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-386: CKTSO's parallel fast factorization shows better scalability than both NICSLU's and PARDISO's parallel factorization, as the average speedups increase with parallelism increasing.
elements.sent-2411-387: When the solvers all use 16 threads, CKTSO is on average 5.9$\times$ and 11.9$\times$ faster than NICSLU and PARDISO, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-388: CKTSO's parallel fast factorization is also compared with CKTSO's parallel re-factorization, and the average speedups are consistently about 0.8.
elements.sent-2411-389: This implies that the proposed fast factorization has similar scalability to re-factorization.
elements.sent-2411-390: In this case, CKTSO is on average 1.21$\times$, 2.74$\times$, 3.71$\times$, and 4.69$\times$ faster than NICSLU(o) when using 1, 4, 8, and 16 threads, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-391: The performance of PARDISO(o) is improved by 2-3$\times$ when using CKTSO's ordering, but CKTSO is still about 4$\times$ faster than PARDISO(o) on average.
elements.sent-2411-392: If a run is selected to perform re-pivoting, the re-pivoting row is randomly selected from $[1,N]$.

elements.sent-2411-410: CKTSO achieves an average speedup of 2.38$\times$ compared with NICSLU in total OP simulation time.
elements.sent-2411-411: Note that besides the sparse solver, there are other core operations (e.g., transistor model evaluation) in a SPICE simulation which cost about half time, so the speedups of the OP simulation time are lower than those of pure LU factorization which are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fspeedup .}
elements.sent-2411-412: As expected, for most NR iterations, CKTSO computes the entire matrix without re-pivoting, which is the main motivation of designing CKTSO.
elements.sent-2411-413: There are indeed some iterations incurring re-pivoting.
elements.sent-2411-414: This usually implies a sharp change in the convergence path of the NR method which is typically caused by significant changes in the matrix values.
elements.sent-2411-415: This explains the necessity of using the proposed fast factorization algorithm to replace pure re-factorization without pivoting, as the latter may generate inaccurate solutions in those iterations that need re-pivoting.

elements.sent-2411-421: Conventionally, in many practical applications, sparse solvers are usually made as black boxes and used as standalone modules.
elements.sent-2411-422: However, if the special features of the matrices involved in the targeted application are taken into account, the solver design can be more dedicated for elevating performance.
elements.sent-2411-423: This paper introduces CKTSO, a parallel sparse linear solver specially optimized for SPICE simulators.
elements.sent-2411-424: By considering the features of the matrix sparsity and the slow change in matrix values in SPICE simulations, the three steps of a sparse direct solver, pre-processing, numerical factorization, and triangular solving, have been elaborated.
elements.sent-2411-425: Both benchmark tests and SPICE simulation tests have revealed the superior performance of CKTSO, in comparison with state-of-the-art solvers.
